mappings.rationale: Directly addresses 811.A on the philosophy of religion, covering the existence and attributes of the divine, the problem of evil, and the extent of human knowledge of God.
Covers questions about the nature of the divine across traditions, including creation, existence of God, and divine attributes, central to 811.A.1-2 and 811.D.3.
Addresses theology as an attempt to understand religious belief, including doctrines of God, revelation, and the role of sacred scriptures across traditions (811.D).
Provides a broad framework for the study and classification of religions, directly relevant to 811.E and 811.A on the nature and characteristics of religion.
Examines belief, faith, and reason in relation to religion, directly relevant to 811.D.1.c on articles of faith and religious creeds, and 811.B on religious experience.
Directly addresses the philosophical problem of free will, one of the special problems explicitly listed in 811.A.1.d.
Addresses the problem of evil and suffering, a special problem of the philosophy of religion listed in 811.A.1.d.
Directly covers atheism as a doctrinal position regarding the divine, the subject of 811.D.3.d.
Directly covers agnosticism, the other position listed alongside atheism in 811.D.3.d, and addresses limits of knowledge of the divine (811.A.1.c).
Addresses religious experience and mystical theology across traditions, covering the nature and varieties of spiritual experience in 811.B and 811.D.2.
Directly addresses the topic of miracles across religious traditions, the subject of 811.D.11.
Directly covers angelology across Judaism, Christianity, Islam, and Hinduism, the specific topic of 811.D.5.
Covers doctrines of evil and religious dualism through the figure of the Devil, relevant to 811.D.3.b on religious dualism and 811.A.1.d on evil.
Directly covers the relationship between religion and science, the subject of 811.G.2.
Addresses myth as a belief system and its relationship to religion, directly relevant to 811.F.1 on myth and mythology.
Provides a comparative survey of creation myths, flood narratives, and deity archetypes across cultures, relevant to 811.F.1 and 811.D.4 on doctrines of creation.
Examines Greek and Roman polytheistic mythological traditions, relevant to 811.F.1 on myth and mythology and 811.D.3.a on polytheism.
Directly covers magic as a system of belief and practice, the subject of 811.F.2.
Directly covers witchcraft as a belief system and practice, the subject of 811.F.3.
Covers polytheism, nature worship, shamanism, and sacred practices, relevant to 811.D.3.a on polytheism and 811.F.4-8 on shamanism, ancestor worship, and nature worship.
Examines superstitious beliefs including astrology and divination, relevant to 811.F.5 on astrology and alchemy.
Explores humanist and secular alternatives to religious belief, relevant to 811.D.3.d on atheism and agnosticism and 811.G on religion and other aspects of experience.
Examines fundamentalism across religious traditions as a phenomenon of religious classification and doctrine, relevant to 811.E and 811.D.
Comparative study of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam as monotheistic faiths, relevant to 811.D.3.c on monotheism and 811.E on classification of religions.
Illustrates the relationship between religion and art through Christian iconography and symbolism, relevant to 811.G.1 on religion and art.
Addresses morality, freedom, and ethical reasoning in relation to religious thought, relevant to 811.G.4 on religion and morality.
Explores the relationship between conscience, morality, and religious duty, relevant to 811.G.4 on religion and morality.
Explores the relationship between religion and public life including church-state separation, relevant to 811.G.3 on religion and society.
Examines religious pluralism and toleration, relevant to 811.G.3 on religion and society.
Explores conflict and peace-building in religious contexts, relevant to 811.G on religion in relation to other aspects of human experience.
Examines concepts of the sacred and its violation across religions, relevant to 811.A on the nature of the divine and 811.G.3 on religion and society.
Engages with fundamental questions about existence, purpose, and meaning that underlie the basic questions of philosophy of religion in 811.A.1.
Covers the theory of interpretation including theological hermeneutics, relevant to 811.D.1.a on the role of Sacred Scriptures.
Addresses fundamental questions about existence, causation, and the nature of reality that underpin debates about the existence of the divine in 811.A.1.a.
Covers existentialist engagement with questions of God, faith, and the death of God (Kierkegaard, Sartre), relevant to 811.A on philosophy of religion.
Covers anthropological approaches to religion, ritual, and belief systems, relevant to 811.C on religious phenomenology and 811.E on classification of religions.
Augustine's theology addresses doctrines of grace, free will, evil, creation, and faith, central topics throughout 811.D.
Aquinas developed proofs for God's existence, doctrines on divine attributes, and natural theology, central to 811.A.1.a and 811.D.3.c on theism.
Spinoza's pantheistic philosophy directly addresses 811.D.3.c.iii on pantheism and panentheism.
Anselm's ontological argument is a landmark in philosophy of religion on the existence of God, directly relevant to 811.A.1.a.
Examines marriage practices across religions including Christianity, Islam, Judaism, and Mormonism, relevant to 811.G.3 on religion and society.
